question:
Authoritative, expert-level catalogue of the FULL regional pantheon of Japanese "gotōchi" (ご当地) ramen — every major regional/local style across Japan, because shops in any city now commonly serve styles from elsewhere (e.g. Hakata tonkotsu and Sapporo miso are available nationwide). Goal: be able to recognize and classify ANY regional style on a menu, anywhere, and write a "where does this bowl sit in the ramen pantheon" narrative. Use correct romaji + kanji and cite authoritative sources.

For EACH style below, give the DEFINING, objectively-verifiable attributes: broth base, tare, aroma oil/fat, noodle type (thickness, wavy/straight, hydration), signature toppings, richness (assari↔kotteri), origin/lineage, and the one-line "tell" that lets you identify it from a menu or photo.

HOKKAIDO: Sapporo miso (札幌味噌 — miso tare, lard, wavy thick noodles, corn/butter/beansprouts, often stir-fried aromatics); Asahikawa shoyu (旭川醤油 — double soup pork+seafood, lard layer to fight cold, wavy low-hydration noodles); Hakodate shio (函館塩 — clear pork-bone shio, the oldest of Hokkaido's three); Muroran curry ramen.

TOHOKU: Kitakata (喜多方 — Fukushima; flat broad wavy "hira-uchi" noodles, niboshi+pork shoyu, asa-ra morning ramen culture); Yamagata (cold ramen 冷やしラーメン; Sakata); Tsugaru niboshi (Aomori, heavy dried-sardine).

KANTO/TOKYO: Tokyo shoyu (東京醤油 — chicken+niboshi dashi, soy tare, wavy noodles, the Rairaiken archetype); Yokohama Iekei (家系 — pork-bone+chicken-oil tonkotsu-shoyu, thick straight noodles, spinach+nori, rice, customizable richness/oil/firmness; Yoshimuraya origin); Hachioji (onion, thick noodles); Sano (佐野 — Tochigi, bamboo-pressed aomori noodles); Ogikubo.

CHUBU/HOKURIKU: Toyama Black (富山ブラック — intensely dark salty shoyu, coarse black pepper, meant with rice); Kyoto (three lineages — see existing notes); Nagoya (Taiwan ramen 台湾ラーメン — spicy minced pork, from Misen); Takayama (高山 — Hida, thin shoyu, double-boiled).

KANSAI/CHUGOKU/SHIKOKU: Wakayama / "Ido" (和歌山 — pork-bone shoyu, "tonkotsu-shoyu"); Tenri stamina (天理スタミナ — Nara, spicy garlic-vegetable-chili); Onomichi (尾道 — chicken+seafood shoyu, floating pork back-fat squares, flat noodles); Fukuyama; Tokushima (徳島 — dark pork-bone shoyu, sukiyaki-like pork, raw egg, "chuukei"); Okayama.

KYUSHU: Hakata (博多 — Fukuoka, thin straight low-hydration noodles, creamy tonkotsu, kaedama+firmness ladder); Nagahama (長浜 — Hakata sibling, even thinner noodles, fishermen origin); Kurume (久留米 — the tonkotsu birthplace, richer); Kumamoto (熊本 — tonkotsu with mayu black-garlic oil and fried garlic chips); Kagoshima (鹿児島 — tonkotsu+chicken/vegetable, semi-cloudy, thicker straight noodles).

Also note any other commonly-served regional styles I'm missing. For each, emphasize what distinguishes it from its neighbors so a documenter can tell e.g. Hakata vs Kurume vs Kagoshima tonkotsu apart, or Sapporo miso vs Asahikawa shoyu. Flag any popular beliefs that are oversimplified or disputed.
summary: Synthesis step was skipped or failed — returning 15 verified claims unmerged.

confirmed.claim: The Japan Ramen Association's official 2025 national gotouchi ramen list contains 185 distinct regional ramen styles (up from 183 in 2024 and 180 in 2023), establishing an authoritative count of recognized regional styles in Japan.
Kurume City is the birthplace of tonkotsu ramen, with its cloudy white broth originating from pork bones accidentally boiled too long at high heat, producing a strong, relatively rich soup fragrance.
confirmed.quote: Kurume City is said to be where tonkotsu ramen originated ... The soup fragrance of Kurume ramen is strong and relatively rich.

confirmed.claim: Nagahama ramen originated at the Hakata fishing port serving market workers, using ultra-thin noodles for fast service, and pioneered the kaedama (replacement noodle) system.
confirmed.quote: These food stalls used ultra-thin noodles in order to be able to feed the workers at the market as quickly as possible ... They also came up with the idea of offering seconds, or replacement noodles for the same bowl of soup.

confirmed.claim: Hakata ramen — the nationally popularized cloudy-white-broth, thin-noodle style — evolved by absorbing techniques from both Kurume (cloudy white broth) and Nagahama (thin noodles and the seconds/kaedama system).
confirmed.quote: Cloudy white pork bone soup with slim noodles

confirmed.claim: Onomichi ramen's broth base is a clear soy-flavored (shoyu) soup made from chicken bone, with the 'noodle-factory style' (製麺所系) also incorporating small fish from the Seto Inland Sea.
confirmed.quote: 鶏ガラで取った澄んだ醤油スープがベース / ダシには瀬戸内の小魚が使われている。

confirmed.claim: The defining 'tell' of Onomichi ramen is liquid fat floating on the surface plus chunks of minced pork back-fat (背脂), which give it notable kotteri richness despite the clear broth.
confirmed.quote: 表面に浮く液状の脂と、背脂をミンチにした塊のせいでこってり感も結構強い。

confirmed.claim: Onomichi noodles are medium-thin, low-hydration, with a flat-cut (平打ち) cross-section.
confirmed.quote: 麺は中細ストレートで少加水...平打ち

confirmed.claim: Kurume City is the birthplace of tonkotsu (pork-bone) ramen, originating with a portable food stall called Nankin Senryo that opened in 1937.
confirmed.quote: It all started in 1937 when a portable food stall called Nankin Senryo opened ... Kurume City ... is the birthplace of tonkotsu ramen

confirmed.claim: Kurume's earliest tonkotsu broth was light-colored, not the cloudy white tonkotsu known today; the white cloudy style only appeared in 1947 when Katsumi Sugino accidentally over-boiled the soup.
confirmed.quote: The white, cloudy style of soup seen today did not appear until 1947 when Katsumi Sugino accidentally left soup cooking on a stovetop

confirmed.claim: The three principal Hokkaido ramen styles are Sapporo (miso), Hakodate (shio), and Asahikawa (shoyu), collectively termed the 'Hokkaido three great ramen' (北海道三大ラーメン); Kushiro is sometimes added to make a 'four great ramen' grouping.
confirmed.quote: 「札幌」「函館」「旭川」のラーメンを総称して「北海道三大ラーメン」、もしくはそれに「釧路」を加えた「四大ラーメン」と呼称されることがある

confirmed.claim: Sapporo miso ramen uses a pork-bone broth with miso tare and floating lard, paired with high-hydration medium-thick wavy noodles (多加水の中太縮れ麺); miso ramen was devised in 1955.
confirmed.quote: 1955年に味噌ラーメンが考案され人気となり

confirmed.claim: Asahikawa shoyu ramen uses a double-soup (fish/pork-bone + chicken/vegetable) with soy tare and a layer of burned lard that forms an insulating oil film against the cold, with low-hydration medium-thin wavy noodles (低加水の中細縮れ麺).

Hakodate shio ramen uses a clear, low-fat pork-bone broth (清湯) with salt tare and high-hydration medium-thin STRAIGHT noodles (多加水の中細ストレート麺) — the straight noodle being distinctive within Hokkaido, which otherwise favors wavy noodles.
confirmed.quote: 多加水の中細ストレート麺

confirmed.claim: Sapporo ramen is defined by a rich miso broth with a layer of lard floating on the surface, a design specifically intended to retain heat in cold climates.
confirmed.quote: ラードが浮いた濃厚な味噌。冷めにくい工夫が特徴。

confirmed.claim: Kitakata ramen (Fukushima) uses a transparent shoyu broth with flat, aged, high-hydration noodles (hira-uchi jukusei taka-sui-men) and is counted among Japan's three major ramen styles.

refuted.claim: Kurume ramen uses noodles that are very white and slightly thick, and many shops do not offer a choice of noodle hardness.
Sano ramen (Tochigi) is characterized by a light/delicate shoyu tare and noodles made by the blue-bamboo hand-pressing method, giving them a flat, floppy 'piropiro' texture.
Tokushima ramen is a tea-colored sweet-and-spicy shoyu-tonkotsu broth, commonly topped with a raw egg and eaten as an accompaniment to rice.

Asahikawa ramen is a soy-sauce (shoyu) style built on a 'double soup' that blends animal-based and seafood-based stocks, with a layer of lard floating on top to retain heat against Hokkaido's cold, paired with low-hydration crimped (wavy) medium-thin noodles.
Hakodate ramen is the shio (salt) style of Hokkaido, defined by a notably clear/high-transparency broth and a light, low-fat, delicate profile served with straight thin noodles.
Kitakata ramen (Fukushima) is distinguished by flat, aged, high-hydration noodles giving a silky-throat, chewy texture, and is tied to an 'asa-ra' (morning ramen) breakfast culture.

Toyama Black ramen is a deeply concentrated soy-sauce broth that is pitch-black and intentionally high in saltiness, designed to be eaten as a seasoning vehicle with white rice (originating as post-WWII laborer food).
Nagaoka-style ramen (長岡系ラーメン, Niigata) is defined by a strong ginger aroma — a distinct lineage not in the original prompt list.

Kagoshima ramen broth is tonkotsu-based but blended with chicken stock, vegetables, dried sardines, kelp and dried mushrooms, producing a milder, only slightly cloudy soup rather than the heavy creamy pure-pork tonkotsu of Hakata.
Kagoshima ramen uses noodles that are thicker than typical (distinguishing it from Hakata/Nagahama's thin straight noodles).
